message.abstract:
<jats:title>ABSTRACT</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objectives</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>With large numbers of COVID-19 patients requiring mechanical ventilation and ventilators possibly being in short supply, in extremis two patients may have to share one ventilator. Careful matching of patient ventilation requirements is necessary. However, good matching is difficult to achieve as lung characteristics can have a wide range and may vary over time. Adding flow restriction to the flow path between ventilator and patient gives the opportunity to control the airway pressure and hence flow and volume individually for each patient. This study aimed to create and validate a simple model for calculating required flow restriction.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design/Setting</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Experimental/Secondary Care</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We created a simple linear resistance-compliance model, termed the BathRC model, of the ventilator tubing system and lung allowing direct calculation of the relationships between pressures, volumes, and required flow restriction. Experimental measurements were made for parameter determination and validation using a clinical ventilator connected to two test lungs. For validation, differing amounts of restriction were introduced into the ventilator circuit.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The BathRC model was able to predict tidal lung volumes with a mean error of 4% (min:1.2%, max:9.3%).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We present a simple model validated model that can be used to estimate required flow restriction for dual patient ventilation. The BathRC model is freely available; this tool is provided to demonstrate that flow restriction can be readily estimated.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>ARTICLE SUMMARY</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Strengths and limitations of this study</jats:p>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Data collected on a clinical system</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Simple model, easy to implement</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Validation performed</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Limited to 2 types of restrictors tested</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Data from only one ventilator type</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: A Simple Method to Estimate Flow Restriction for Dual Ventilation of Dissimilar Patients: The BathRC Model

reference.unstructured: Joint Statement on Multiple Patients Per Ventilator: Anesthetic Patient Safety Foundation, 2020.

Beitler JR , Kallet R , Kacmarek R , et al. Ventilator Sharing Protocol: Dual-Patient Ventilation with a Single Mechanical Ventilator for Use during Critical Ventilator Shortages Columbia University 2020.
Tronstad C , Martinsen T , Olsen M , et al. Splitting one ventilator for multiple patients -- a technical assessment. arXiv 2020;arXiv:2003.12349
Pinson H . A better way of connecting multiple patients to a single ventilator, 2020:https://medium.com/@pinsonhannah/a-better-way-of-connecting-multiple-patients-to-a-single-ventilator-fa9cf42679c6.
Magee P . Mathematical modelling and clinical testing of low flow breathing systems [PhD]. University of Bath, 2014.
